title: Rubio confirms Trump at NATO Ankara summit, Şimşek defends disinflation, Iraq triples Ceyhan exports
subhead: Trump's confirmed attendance at the July NATO summit in Ankara puts Turkey at center of alliance reckoning. Ankara's finance minister insists disinflation stays on track despite war shocks. Iraq's pivot to Ceyhan pipeline gives Turkey's energy-hub ambitions a concrete boost.

sections.heading: Trump confirms NATO summit attendance, Rubio calls Ankara meeting 'most important in NATO history'
sections.body:
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio told a congressional hearing on Wednesday that President Donald Trump will attend the NATO heads-of-state summit in Ankara on July 7-8, ending months of uncertainty about whether the U.S. president would show up at all. Trump had repeatedly called NATO a 'paper tiger' and threatened withdrawal over European allies' refusal to support U.S. military operations against Iran, including denying use of bases and airspace and declining naval support to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Rubio framed the stakes starkly: 'I think the next meeting of NATO in Turkey in July is probably the most important meeting in NATO's history, because there are some things here that need to be cleared up and fixed.' He acknowledged Trump's frustration that some members blocked U.S. use of military bases during the Iran crisis but said the alliance would address these grievances at the summit.

For Ankara, Trump's confirmed presence is a diplomatic win. Turkey has navigated the Iran war by refusing basing rights to the U.S. while maintaining its NATO membership — a posture that irritated Washington but preserved Turkey's regional maneuvering room. Hosting Trump on Turkish soil gives Erdoğan a platform to position Turkey as a necessary interlocutor rather than a reluctant ally. Reuters reported in April that NATO was considering ending annual summits partly to avoid a tense encounter with Trump in 2028 — making this year's gathering potentially the last regular summit for some time.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's attendance transforms the Ankara summit from a routine gathering into a showdown over burden-sharing and alliance purpose. Turkey's refusal to host U.S. bases during the Iran war will be on the agenda, and Ankara gets to play host rather than defendant.

sections.heading: Şimşek tells investors disinflation on track, targets mid-20s inflation by year-end
sections.body:
Treasury and Finance Minister Mehmet Şimşek told investors at the Nomura Investment Forum Asia 2026 that Ankara's commitment to disinflation remains 'firm,' projecting year-end inflation in the mid-20s even amid the energy shock from the Iran war. 'Even in a year of major shocks, inflation is expected to continue falling,' he said, according to Daily Sabah.

April's annual inflation stood at 32.37% with a 4.18% monthly increase — a slowdown in the disinflation trend after the Iran war sent energy prices sharply higher. May data is due Friday. Şimşek argued the shock may slow delivery but won't change direction: 'Shocks may slow the pace of the program's delivery, but they are unlikely to change the direction of travel.'

On fiscal performance, Şimşek cited a track record of reducing the budget deficit from 5.1% of GDP in 2023 to 2.9% in 2025 and said the government remains on track to keep the deficit below 3% of GDP in 2026 even after deploying fiscal space to cushion oil prices through a sliding-scale mechanism. The current account deficit is expected around 3% of GDP, below its long-term average, supported by resilient exports and supply-chain reconfiguration. He also highlighted a 12.5% corporate tax rate for manufacturers and full tax exemption on services exports as part of Ankara's FDI attraction framework.
sections.whyItMatters: Şimşek is making the case that the post-2023 orthodoxy holds despite war shocks. The mid-20s target assumes $90/barrel oil; if Hormuz stays closed and prices spike further, that math breaks. Friday's May inflation data will be the first real test.

sections.heading: Iraq triples Ceyhan pipeline exports as Hormuz closure forces Baghdad northward
sections.body:
Iraq's Prime Minister Ali Falih Al-Zaidi directed oil companies in Kurdistan to resume operations on Thursday, as OPEC's second-largest producer scrambles to compensate for the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. According to Oilprice.com, Iraq plans to triple pipeline exports through Ceyhan to 770,000 bpd, up from roughly 220,000 bpd currently, within three months.

The move is existential for Baghdad. Iraq's southern exports through Basrah have crumbled since the Strait of Hormuz closed over three months ago, and the prime minister's office acknowledged 'significant damage' from the disruption. Kurdistan's oil, shipped via pipeline to the Turkish Mediterranean port of Ceyhan, has become a lifeline. Norway-based DNO, one of the top international producers in Kurdistan, restarted field operations on April 9 after the ceasefire and relaunched an eight-well drilling campaign.

For Turkey, this is a concrete vindication of its energy-corridor pitch. Ceyhan is suddenly handling not just Kazakh crude rerouted from Russian Black Sea ports (which Ukraine's drone strikes on Novorossiysk have disrupted) but now surging Iraqi volumes as well. An Institude analysis published Thursday noted that the conflicts around Turkey are 'quietly redrawing the region's energy geography,' with Ceyhan emerging as the primary alternative outlet for Middle Eastern crude.
sections.whyItMatters: Ceyhan's throughput could nearly quadruple if Baghdad hits its target. That translates directly into transit revenue and geopolitical relevance for Turkey. But it also makes Ceyhan a strategic target in any escalation.

sections.heading: Erdoğan says Iran war 'cements' Turkey's energy role, touts renewable push
sections.body:
Speaking at the inauguration of renewable energy projects completed in 2025, President Erdoğan said the Middle East conflict has 'cemented Türkiye's critical role in the global energy supply.' He described energy security as 'not only a development issue but also a matter of sovereignty and national security,' according to Daily Sabah.

Erdoğan cited hard numbers: imported sources account for about 57% of Türkiye's energy supply with an annual import bill of roughly $60 billion. Total installed electricity capacity reached 125,410 MW by end-April, with renewables at 62.5%. Solar alone contributed 26,770 MW. He reaffirmed the National Energy Plan target of raising combined solar and wind capacity from 40 GW to 120 GW by 2035, requiring roughly $80 billion in investment.

On the gas side, Erdoğan pointed to Azerbaijani gas flowing into Syria since August 2025 and the prospect of exporting Turkmen gas through Azerbaijan and Turkey. But the Institude analysis notes structural limits: the Turkmenistan-Turkey swap deal relies on Iranian infrastructure subject to sanctions and underinvestment, and a Trans-Caspian Pipeline remains blocked by Russian and Iranian opposition to Caspian seabed delimitation.
sections.whyItMatters: Ankara is selling the Hormuz crisis as proof of concept for its energy-hub vision. The Ceyhan volumes are real. But the 57% import dependency and $60 billion annual bill underscore how far Turkey remains from energy sovereignty, no matter how central its transit corridors become.

sections.heading: Fidan in Jakarta: Turkey-Indonesia deepen defense and Palestine diplomacy
sections.body:
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan met Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto in Jakarta on Wednesday at Prabowo's direct invitation, according to Antara News. The two discussed expanding cooperation in defense, energy, transportation, and halal food sectors, working toward a bilateral trade target of $10 billion. Al Jazeera reported the talks also covered defense industry cooperation.

On Palestine, both sides agreed to strengthen cooperation in returning international attention to the Palestinian cause, which Indonesian Foreign Minister Sugiono said had been diverted by the U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran. Prabowo also expressed appreciation for Turkey's role in securing the release of nine Indonesian nationals detained by Israeli forces during the interception of the Global Sumud Flotilla 2.0 humanitarian convoy last month.

The visit fits a pattern: Ankara has been systematically courting Global South partners who share its position on Palestine and its reluctance to join the U.S.-led Iran campaign. Indonesia, as the world's largest Muslim-majority country and a fellow 'strategic autonomy' practitioner, is a natural partner. The Conversation notes that Turkey is among the countries leveraging the 'credible ability to say no' to Washington as an organizing principle of its foreign policy.
sections.whyItMatters: The Jakarta visit is about building a coalition of countries that stayed out of the Iran war and want Palestine back on the agenda. The defense cooperation angle — including potential drone and naval exports — gives Turkey commercial leverage alongside diplomatic alignment.

sections.heading: CHP crisis: Özel rallies thousands in Ankara as Kılıçdaroğlu digs in at party HQ
sections.body:
Thousands marched through Ankara on Saturday in support of ousted CHP leader Özgür Özel, according to DW. The rally followed the May 21 ruling by the 36th Civil Chamber of the Ankara Regional Court of Appeal declaring the 2023 CHP congress that elected Özel as 'absolutely null' (mutlak butlan), provisionally reinstating former leader Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu. Özel led supporters to Atatürk's mausoleum and called for an immediate party congress, rejecting the idea of an internal dispute: 'This matter concerns Recep Tayyip Erdoğan and the people.'

Meanwhile, Kılıçdaroğlu spoke at CHP headquarters marking Eid al-Adha and vowed to hold a 'clean, completely transparent' party congress. Eurotopics reports that the legal basis of the ruling is contested: Article 21 of the Political Parties Act designates the Supreme Electoral Council (YSK) as the only competent authority for party congress validity, yet the court ruled under the Civil Code. Even AKP founding figure Bülent Arınç called the decision 'wrong in terms of jurisdiction, remit and procedural law,' according to Turkish daily Karar.

The crisis is now a standoff: Özel commands street support and refuses to recognize the ruling; Kılıçdaroğlu occupies the party headquarters with a court order behind him. Eurotopics quotes Birgün daily warning that 'the police search of the CHP headquarters and the de facto takeover of its leadership is the culmination of the coup process that began on 19 March' — referring to the arrest of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu in 2025.
sections.whyItMatters: The CHP is split between a leader with popular support and no legal standing, and a leader with a court order and no street mandate. The longer this persists, the weaker the opposition enters the 2028 election cycle — which is precisely the accusation Özel and his supporters level at the government.

sections.heading: HAVA SOJ electronic warfare jet shown in clearest imagery yet
sections.body:
New imagery of Turkey's HAVA SOJ airborne electronic warfare aircraft, released in a Ministry of National Defense video marking the 115th anniversary of the Turkish Air Force, reveals the modified Bombardier Global 6000 airframe in unprecedented detail, according to The Aviationist and The War Zone. The aircraft's external EW payloads — including a ventral canoe fairing, dorsal SATCOM dome, wingtip-mounted ELINT pods resembling those on the U.S. EA-18G Growler, and Leonardo Miysis DIRCM — are now clearly visible.

TAI is responsible for airframe modification and integration of Aselsan's EW payload. Four HAVA SOJ aircraft are under development, with deliveries expected to begin in 2026. The platform makes Turkey one of few air forces in the Mediterranean and Levant with a standoff jamming capability, alongside the U.S. (EA-18G), Italy (EA-37B Compass Call, on order), and France (Archange).

The program, launched in 2018, had been delayed but the new imagery suggests it is nearing operational status. The airframe still carries a flight data probe, indicating flight testing is ongoing.
sections.whyItMatters: Standoff electronic warfare is a force multiplier that few regional powers possess. If HAVA SOJ reaches operational status this year, it significantly upgrades Turkey's ability to suppress adversary air defenses without committing manned fighters to contested airspace — a relevant capability given the density of air defense systems on Turkey's southern borders.

bottomLine: Three storylines converged today that define Turkey's moment: Trump confirmed he'll show up in Ankara for what Rubio called the most important NATO summit in the alliance's history — putting Turkey's refusal to host U.S. Iran-war bases squarely on the agenda. Şimşek went to bat for the disinflation program, promising mid-20s inflation by year-end even as oil sits above $90 and May data drops Friday. And Iraq's order to triple Ceyhan pipeline exports gave Turkey's energy-hub pitch its most tangible boost yet. Meanwhile, the CHP leadership crisis remains deadlocked — Özel has the streets, Kılıçdaroğlu has the court order, and neither side is backing down. The opposition enters the 2028 cycle fragmented unless the YSK or a higher court resolves the jurisdictional mess.
sources: Cyprus Mail, Daily Sabah, Oilprice.com, The Aviationist, The War Zone, DW, Eurotopics, Antara News, Al Jazeera, The Conversation, Institude, BBC, Reuters, IEA
